speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Montreal. It's a nice city.

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- oh, I was first-- I was in Montreal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It's-- the people are more--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and this is tape four.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. This is tape number four, and we're beginning-- can we go back? You--

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- tape number four, and it's Sarah Salamon. And Sarah, you were-- you had mentioned your giving testimony in Nuremburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you give testimony about Mengele there, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no-- I don't remember. Yes, I mentioned Mengele there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were-- you were questioned about Mengele. And-- and where he was when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah. He says, I didn't. I told him, in-- in-- in-- by the table. I says, you know, Dr. Mengele, you changed a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: From when he was in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. His finger just hitting in the air-- that was life or death-- thousands of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you met him again where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] --oh the-- the first when we go out from Auschwitz-- what I mentioned.

speaker: interviewee
text: Gross-Rosen, yeah-- there. Because the people got frozen in the feet. And he had to make the operate-- but he is quick in his hand. He must be a good doctor. He's a surgeon. And how he talked to us-- kinder help one the other. This will be any day finish. And I, with my big mouth, I says, look who's talking-- right by the table. And he said, 'Look at me.' He says, 'Listen, kleine. Shut your mouth.' I says, 'I'm sorry, doctor.' I don't think so that I hurt him with this.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you spoke German, as well as Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you speak Yiddish as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too good, but I learned. I speak very well Yiddish, now. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: After the war, with your husband, your-- your second husband--

speaker: interviewee
text: To Sweden, yeah. When Benny was two weeks old, I moved to Sweden. Because my husband got a auntie in the States, and she says, get out from-- from Deutschland. And if you will be no matter where, then I can take you here.

speaker: interviewee
text: But he found there was already a couple cousins in Montreal. And I says, you know what, Dave? Don't count it of nothing. What come first, then we are going. And it was a couple years we were in Sweden. You were in-- in Sweden for a few years?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your husband do there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of clothes-- ladies and men. You know, Crete? Here, the-- he worked there ten years, like a designer. He make for Queen Elizabeth. Every year when she came here, she came to David, shaked hand, and she ordered some fur and clothes. But she says to-- to Crete-- what I wanted, this designer should do for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Sweden, how did you get from Sweden to Canada?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the ship?

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and did you have somebody who sponsored you here? Was there a relative here that brought you over?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. One is dead-- the oldest. She was 90-year-old-- a nice lady.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Helen-- what was her name? Linjin. And she has one sister in Montreal. She lives on Smart Street.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her husband was a-- a good mink specialist-- fur coats-- the best in-- in Montreal.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you were brought over here to-- you came directly to Canada.

speaker: interviewee
text: Directly to Montreal, with the ship-- black like Negroes. It was so dirty in the train, because we came with a ship. And then we had to stay over a day in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Halifax, yeah. I am telling you, I will never forget [LAUGHS] this-- this day-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] --such a dirty-- but now, they says it's very nice in Halifax.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you came to Montreal directly?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Of Blumen-- what is the street? Yeah, the-- my husband's cousin, she got a beautiful apartment there-- five rooms. And you know that she give away us her bedroom. And she slept on the floor. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And a month ago, she died. But she was here in-- when was her granddaughter's wedding? We-- we want to thank you ever so much for sharing your story with us. You have some pictures you would like to share with us. We'll do those now. And you also have your-- name-- your number on your arm.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'd appreciate it if you would let us show that as well, afterward, at the end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because some people say, why you don't take out? Now they can take out. I says, if they would-- can take out from my heart? This is what I went through! I would do it. But-- [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Who's in the picture, when the picture-- wait one minute-- not yet-- and when the picture was taken.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And just tell us who is in the picture. That's--

speaker: interviewee
text: It's me. This is the first.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left, mine mommy's brother's daughter, Adella.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so now you can-- are you on?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, will you tell us the-- who's in the picture, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mine cousin, Adella-- Adella Fogelgaron, and Henry, mine son, and me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. OK. OK, starting from the left, that's-- on the left hand side, that's you?

speaker: interviewer
text: And then in the middle is your son, Henry?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- and-- and is this the son that was--

speaker: interviewer
text: And then on the right is?

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. OK, can you tell us who's in the picture?

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this the child you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Left on the-- on the steps in the church.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And this was before the-- just at the beginning of the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll do the pictures. We'll do some salami sandwiches.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us who this is?

speaker: interviewer
text: And approximately what year did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, you-- you ask me such question.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Can you tell us-- this is-- this is--

speaker: interviewer
text: And-- and he's the father of--

speaker: interviewer
text: And all three of them perished in the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and me.

speaker: interviewee
text: David Salamon. And this is when I born, Benny, our son. And-- and he came with a cameraman and made pictures from my bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was-- where did this happen?

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your husband go through the war, as well?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us about the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my husband and my son.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry. Could we just start again? Just--

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You know, your-- your-- your microphone is over here. And if you put your hand over there-- [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When you say this is my husband, you say this is my husband, David--

speaker: interviewer
text: Litwin. You know, give it-- David Salamon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So give the whole name. And my son, Benny Salamon.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is-- this is mine husband, Dave Salamon. And this is our son, Benny Salamon. What they are both-- both not alive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And Benny was getting what here? His--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did Benny have something before, a degree before that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah-- doctor degree. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: At my son's wedding. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us about this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my son and his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Lisa Esther Salamon. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us a little bit about her?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is the third year in medicine, in McMaster University. And she's the one from the best students. I showed you her. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Mindy Salamon, mine granddaughter, second one. She will go in the fall in University of York, take law. She has to finish this-- what the father started. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, can you tell us the--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Jessica, mine third granddaughter. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] She will be 12 in June, in the same day what Benny's born, the 13 of June. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you tell us what happened on your arm? You have a number on your arm. How did that--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz they make this, when just we came in. They just like you give cattle a number, they give us, too. And the blood was running, because they pick with a some-- thing-- I don't know. That was hurting very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: 6,300-- no-- 63,959. And this is the Jewish mark. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
